skill.name: mcp-builder
skill.description: Deprecated MCP builder skill for creating Model Context Protocol servers with the mcp-use framework. It guides tools, resources, prompts, response helpers, URI templates, and React widgets. Use the replacement mcp-app-builder when available.
skill.summary: Guides MCP server creation with mcp-use tools, resources, prompts, and widgets.
skill.author: mcp-use

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many high-severity alerts, but review found most are false positives from Markdown fences, Zod .describe() examples, and license text. The skill is documentation-focused and shows no confirmed malicious intent, but it can guide generated MCP servers toward environment-variable, filesystem, and external API patterns that need security review.

medium_findings.title: API Key in External Fetch Example
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE as risky guidance, not confirmed malicious behavior. The reference reads WEATHER_API_KEY from process.env and places it in a fetch query string, which generated servers could expose through logs or proxies.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment variable and external fetch are shown directly in the same example. It is documentation, so the risk is guidance quality rather than active exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Unvalidated File Resource Template Example
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE as risky guidance. The resource template shows file://{path} flowing into readFile(path) without validation, which generated MCP resources could turn into arbitrary file disclosure.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The example directly maps a URI path parameter into a file read. It is not executable in this skill, but the generated pattern is clearly unsafe without path allowlisting.

medium_findings.title: Deprecated Skill Recommends External Install Command
medium_findings.description: NEEDS_REVIEW. The skill tells users to install the replacement with npx, which is a legitimate migration path but can execute remote package-manager code if followed.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command is explicit and user-facing, but it points to the same project replacement rather than an unknown endpoint. Human review should confirm package provenance.

low_findings.title: Markdown Fences Misidentified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The Ruby or shell backtick alerts are caused by Markdown code fences and inline code in documentation, not runtime command execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are Markdown examples or fenced code blocks. No executable script file or shell invocation is present there.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto Alerts Are String-Match False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The weak cryptography alerts match words such as description, .describe(), and license text, not cryptographic algorithms or hashing calls.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review found Zod schema description methods and legal prose, not MD5, SHA1, DES, or crypto APIs.

low_findings.title: LLM Sampling Example Uses Raw Tool Input
low_findings.description: NEEDS_REVIEW. The documentation shows ctx.sample receiving raw input data for summarization, which may disclose sensitive user content to an LLM during generated server operation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The example is legitimate MCP sampling guidance, but it lacks warnings about sensitive content filtering. Actual risk depends on generated server use.

dangerous_patterns.title: API Key in External Fetch Example
dangerous_patterns.description: TRUE_POSITIVE as risky guidance, not confirmed malicious behavior. The reference reads WEATHER_API_KEY from process.env and places it in a fetch query string, which generated servers could expose through logs or proxies.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The environment variable and external fetch are shown directly in the same example. It is documentation, so the risk is guidance quality rather than active exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unvalidated File Resource Template Example
dangerous_patterns.description: TRUE_POSITIVE as risky guidance. The resource template shows file://{path} flowing into readFile(path) without validation, which generated MCP resources could turn into arbitrary file disclosure.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The example directly maps a URI path parameter into a file read. It is not executable in this skill, but the generated pattern is clearly unsafe without path allowlisting.

dangerous_patterns.title: LLM Sampling Example Uses Raw Tool Input
dangerous_patterns.description: NEEDS_REVIEW. The documentation shows ctx.sample receiving raw input data for summarization, which may disclose sensitive user content to an LLM during generated server operation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The example is legitimate MCP sampling guidance, but it lacks warnings about sensitive content filtering. Actual risk depends on generated server use.

content.value_statement: MCP server projects need clear structure for tools, resources, prompts, and widgets. This deprecated skill provides mcp-use patterns and examples for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use MCP tools, resources, prompts, and widgets.
Provides mcp-use server patterns for TypeScript projects.
Shows response helper usage for text, objects, markdown, media, errors, and widgets.
Documents URI template patterns for parameterized resources.
Guides React widget structure with metadata, props, state, and tool calls.
Includes manual evaluation scenarios for common MCP server requests.
limitations: The skill is deprecated and recommends mcp-app-builder when available.
It provides guidance only and does not run or validate generated servers.
Some examples use mock data or placeholder APIs.
Generated servers still require security review for secrets, files, and network access.

use_cases.title: Bootstrap an MCP Server
use_cases.description: Create a new mcp-use server with focused tools, resources, prompts, and response helpers.

use_cases.title: Add Interactive MCP Widgets
use_cases.description: Design React widgets that display tool output and call other MCP tools from the UI.

use_cases.title: Review MCP Architecture Choices
use_cases.description: Decide whether a feature should be a tool, resource, prompt, or widget before implementation.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Simple Tool
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an mcp-use tool for this task: [describe task]. Include inputs, validation, response helper choice, and error handling.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Server Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this MCP app idea: [describe app]. Decide which parts should be tools, resources, prompts, or widgets.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Widget Flow
prompt_templates.scenario: Create an interactive UI for tool output
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an mcp-use widget for [workflow]. Include widget props, state needs, tool calls, and loading behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Harden a Generated Server
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this mcp-use server design for security. Focus on environment variables, file access, external APIs, destructive tools, and user input validation.

output_examples.output: A plan for weather lookup tools, a visual widget, environment variable handling, and graceful missing-key errors.

output_examples.input: Add a resource for user profiles.
output_examples.output: A resource design with a stable URI pattern, typed parameters, read-only behavior, and a structured object response.

output_examples.input: My widget shows missing values while loading.
output_examples.output: A diagnosis that the widget renders before tool completion, plus guidance to show a loading state first.

best_practices: Start with architecture decisions before writing MCP server code.
Describe every schema field and validate user input tightly.
Review generated file, network, and secret handling before deployment.
anti_patterns: Using one broad tool when focused tools would be clearer.
Passing unvalidated URI path parameters into file reads.
Putting API keys in URLs or generated logs.

faq.question: Is this skill still current?
faq.answer: No. The skill says it is deprecated and recommends mcp-app-builder when that skill is available.

faq.question: Does it create complete MCP servers?
faq.answer: It provides patterns and examples. The assistant still generates and edits the project files.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Can it build visual MCP widgets?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents React widget files, widget metadata, props, state, and tool-call integration.

faq.question: Does it handle API keys safely?
faq.answer: It shows environment-variable patterns, but generated code must avoid exposing secrets in URLs or logs.

faq.question: Should generated servers be reviewed?
faq.answer: Yes. Review filesystem access, external APIs, destructive actions, and prompt or sampling behavior before publishing.
